data.title: Keck Seng Investments HK One Off Gain Drives Earnings And Tests Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong) (SEHK:184) has opened FY 2025 with first half revenue of HK$772.6 million and basic EPS of HK$0.03, setting a more muted tone against a backdrop of previously higher earnings. Over recent periods, the company has reported revenue of HK$809.0 million and EPS of HK$0.26 in 1H 2024, followed by revenue of HK$883.0 million and EPS of HK$0.50 in 2H 2024, before arriving at the current 1H 2025 levels. With a 15.9% net profit margin over the last 12 months and a HK$221.0 million one off gain influencing earnings, investors are likely to focus on how underlying profitability is holding up beneath the headline figures.

See our full analysis for Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong).

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the prevailing market narratives and where the data may challenge what investors think they know about Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong).

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM profit margin edges up to 15.9%

  • Over the last 12 months, Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong) reported a 15.9% net profit margin, slightly above the earlier 15.1% level on trailing figures of HK$1,616.4 million in revenue and HK$257.5 million in net income excluding extra items.
  • What stands out for bullish investors is that this margin picture sits alongside five year earnings growth of 69.9% per year, yet the most recent year on year earnings growth was only 0.6%, which:
    • Supports the bullish idea of a strong long term earnings record, given the very high multi year growth rate alongside a mid teens margin.
    • Challenges a simple bullish read through for the latest period, because the 0.6% recent growth is far below that long run average even though margins ticked up.

HK$221.0 million one off gain shapes earnings quality

  • The trailing 12 month earnings include a HK$221.0 million one off gain, which is material relative to the HK$257.5 million net income figure used in the margin calculation.
  • Critics highlight this one off as a pressure point for the bullish story, and the numbers give that argument some weight because:
    • A large portion of the HK$257.5 million trailing net income is linked to that HK$221.0 million non recurring gain rather than to ongoing operations.
    • 1H 2025 net income excluding extra items was HK$11.0 million compared with HK$87.3 million in 1H 2024 and HK$168.5 million in 2H 2024, which shows how different reported profit can look once one offs are stripped out.

P/E of 3.1x versus DCF fair value of HK$7.94

  • The shares trade on a P/E of 3.1x compared with 59.7x for peers and 14.7x for the Hong Kong hospitality industry, while the DCF fair value is HK$7.94 against a current share price of HK$2.33.
  • Supporters of a bullish, value oriented view point to this gap, yet the same data also prompts questions, because:
    • The low P/E and the roughly HK$5.61 difference between DCF fair value and the share price line up with the history of strong multi year earnings growth, which heavily supports the bullish case that the stock screens as inexpensive.
    • At the same time, the unstable dividend record and the HK$221.0 million one off gain affecting trailing earnings give bears a concrete basis to argue that part of the apparent discount may reflect concerns over how consistent those earnings and cash returns might be.

To see how other investors interpret this mix of low P/E, one off gains, and profit margins, and how that feeds into different bull and bear storylines, it is worth reading the wider community discussion around Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong) 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Keck Seng Investments (Hong Kong).

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Oncolytics Biotech (ONCY) After Recent Trial Progress News?
data.description:
• Wondering if Oncolytics Biotech at around US$0.95 is offering value or just extra risk? This article breaks down what that price tag could really mean for you.
  • The stock is roughly flat year to date with a 3.4% decline. It has returned 54.7% over the last year, which can raise questions about how much potential or risk might already be reflected in the price.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Oncolytics Biotech's progress as a clinical stage cancer therapy developer and the implications of its ongoing trial updates for funding and partnerships. This news provides useful background when you think about why the share price has moved over different time frames.
  • Right now the company scores a value score of 2 out of 6. Next, you will see how traditional valuation tools line up for Oncolytics Biotech and then a more complete way to think about value later in the article.

Oncolytics Biotech scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Oncolytics Biotech Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value.

For Oncolytics Biotech, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The company currently reports last twelve month free cash flow of CA$26.48 million in outflows. Analyst inputs and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to projected free cash flows moving to CA$169.4 million by 2030, with a series of negative and then positive figures along the way as shown in the ten year forecast path.

Aggregating these CA$ cash flows and discounting them back gives an estimated intrinsic value of US$87.24 per share. Compared with a share price around US$0.95, this DCF output implies the stock is 98.9% undervalued based solely on these projections and assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Oncolytics Biotech is undervalued by 98.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 55 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Oncolytics Biotech.

Approach 2: Oncolytics Biotech Price vs Book

For companies that are still building toward consistent profitability, the price to book, or P/B, ratio is often a practical way to think about valuation because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of the business on its balance sheet.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” P/B multiple, while slower growth and higher risk usually line up with a lower multiple. That is why simply comparing one stock’s P/B to another’s can be misleading if their growth profiles and risk levels are very different.

Oncolytics Biotech currently trades on a P/B ratio of 1,228.17x. This is far above the Biotechs industry average of 2.45x and also above the peer group average of 4.38x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is designed to adjust for factors like earnings growth, profit margins, size, sector and specific risks, so it aims to be a more tailored benchmark than a straight comparison to sector or peers. Result: ABOUT RIGHT

data.title: DPC Dash (SEHK:1405) Margin Improvement Challenges Caution Around Store Expansion Narrative
data.description:
DPC Dash (SEHK:1405) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, with first half revenue of C¥2.6b and basic EPS of C¥0.50, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of C¥5.4b and EPS of C¥1.08 that highlight how the top line and per share earnings are tracking. Over the past few reporting periods, revenue has moved from C¥2.0b in 1H 2024 to C¥2.3b in 2H 2024 and then to C¥2.6b in 1H 2025, while EPS has gone from C¥0.08 to C¥0.34 and then to C¥0.50. This sets up a story where investors will focus on how these earnings convert into durable margin performance.

See our full analysis for DPC Dash.

With the latest figures in hand, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely shared growth and risk narratives that have built up around DPC Dash.

See what the community is saying about DPC Dash

Advertisement

Margins and Net Profit Catch Up to Store Growth

  • On a trailing basis, net income excluding extra items is C¥141.9 million on C¥5.4b of revenue, which works out to a 2.6% net margin compared with 1.3% a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative highlights operational efficiencies and scale benefits as a key support for margins, and the higher trailing margin aligns with that, while store level staff costs at 28% of revenue and rider expenses mentioned in the cautious view show that wage and delivery costs remain an important swing factor for how much of future revenue turns into profit.
    • For example, semi annual net income excluding extra items went from C¥10.9 million in 1H 2024 to C¥65.9 million in 1H 2025 alongside revenue moving from C¥2.0b to C¥2.6b, which is consistent with better profit conversion on a larger base.
    • At the same time, the bearish narrative flags that higher delivery penetration and aggregator commissions could limit further uplift in group EBITDA margin, so the recent 2.6% net margin still sits against those cost headwinds.

Same Store Sales Slip While Network Expands

  • Total restaurants increased from 914 in 1H 2024 to 1,198 in 1H 2025, while same restaurant sales moved from 3.6% growth to a 1% decline and management commentary notes a 5.3% decline in average daily sales per store to C¥12,428.
  • Bears argue that aggressive expansion into non Tier 1 cities, which now contribute 58.8% of revenue, risks weaker unit economics over time, and the combination of a 1% decline in same store sales and lower average daily sales per store directly reflects that concern.
    • The plan to add 307 net openings in 2025 and 350 more in 2026, alongside a reported 12 month payback period for new stores, is framed as attractive today, but bears caution that slower ramp in newer cohorts could stretch those payback periods and weigh on margins.
    • Rider costs and aggregator commissions have already pushed store level staff costs to 28% of revenue, so if same store sales remain under pressure while fixed costs tied to a larger store base keep rising, the cautious view sees a risk that profit growth lags the pace of store openings.
On these store productivity and cost trends, skeptics see plenty of room for debate about how robust earnings will look a few years out, which is exactly what the more cautious narrative unpacks in detail. 🐻 DPC Dash Bear Case

Fast EPS Growth Meets Premium Valuation

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew very quickly, with reported year over year earnings growth of 157.1% and five year annualized earnings growth of 76.2%, yet the shares trade at a P/E of 41.5x compared with peer and Hong Kong Hospitality averages of 21.5x and 14.7x, and a current price of HK$50.75 sits above a DCF fair value of HK$41.58.
  • Consensus narrative leans on strong forecast earnings growth of 36.6% per year and revenue growth of 19.4% per year to justify a higher multiple, and the trailing margin lift to 2.6% and the C¥1.08 of trailing twelve month EPS support that growth story, while the gap between the current price and both the 41.5x P/E premium and the HK$41.58 DCF fair value shows why some investors may see valuation risk if growth or margins do not track expectations.
    • Analysts collectively point to an implied price target of about HK$108.69, which is more than double the current HK$50.75 price and reflects how much of the recent earnings trajectory they are extrapolating.
    • Against that, the fact that the share price already sits above DCF fair value and that the P/E is roughly 2x peers means investors are paying a clear premium today while weighing how durable the current growth and profitability trends will be.
With earnings growth, margins and valuation all pulling in different directions, bulls and bears each have plenty of numbers to point to in building their case, which is what the bullish narrative section walks through in more detail. 🐂 DPC Dash Bull Case

data.title: Netflix Backs Away From Warner Deal To Focus On Core Growth Choices
data.description:
• Netflix, ticker NasdaqGS:NFLX, has walked away from a proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery.
  • The move reflects a choice to focus on internal execution and selective investments instead of a large media merger.
  • This decision keeps attention on Netflix's core streaming business, advertising build out, and product roadmap.
  • Investors are assessing what this means for Netflix's growth plans and position in the broader streaming market.

Netflix runs a global streaming platform that competes for time, attention, and content against other large media and tech groups. The sector continues to be shaped by rising content costs, consolidation among studios, and a push toward ad supported tiers as customers look for flexible pricing. In that context, deciding against a mega deal keeps Netflix's current model and priorities in clear focus.

For you as an investor, this raises questions about how NasdaqGS:NFLX aims to grow without a transformative acquisition in the near term. Future updates around content spending, advertising progress, and product features will help clarify how management intends to deploy capital and sustain its position in the streaming market.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Netflix by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Netflix.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 3 things going right for Netflix that every investor should see.

Walking away from Warner Bros. Discovery signals that Netflix is prioritizing disciplined capital allocation over headline-grabbing scale. Instead of absorbing a complex studio and its streaming assets, management is keeping attention on areas it already operates in, such as content partnerships, its ad-supported tier, and product improvements. Recent moves, including the Warner Music Group first-look deal, the BTS live concert test, and premium ad initiatives in markets like Canada, all point to an approach that uses partnerships and internal capabilities rather than a single large acquisition to support growth. For you as an investor, the question is whether this focus on execution and targeted deals can address concerns that previously surfaced around content spending, integration risk, and slower net additions in mature regions.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Netflix Narrative

  • Stepping back from a large Warner acquisition aligns with the narrative that Netflix is using its global reach, ad tech, and localized content deals to grow, rather than relying on transformational M&A to drive future earnings.
  • The earlier bid for Warner Bros. Discovery raised questions in the narrative about rising content costs and competition, and this reversal highlights that those pressures are still present if future content investments or partnerships do not translate into sustained engagement.
  • The decision to walk away, while leaning into music content, live events, AI-powered tools, and ad-supported products, sharpens some narrative themes but may not fully reflect how Netflix balances buybacks, targeted acquisitions like InterPositive, and organic investment when capital is constrained.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Netflix to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ By choosing not to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery while competitors like Disney, Amazon and a potential Paramount Warner combination build larger content libraries, Netflix may face tougher negotiations for licensed content and more pressure to keep spending on originals.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have already highlighted at least 1 risk, including insider activity, and the decision to keep investing heavily in content, live events and AI tools without the scale of a major studio could limit flexibility if viewing habits keep tilting toward gaming and user-generated platforms.
  • 🎁 Avoiding a complex Warner integration removes execution and regulatory uncertainty, allowing management to keep refining a business that already benefits from high EBITDA and free cash flow margins, and to continue buybacks and focused investments.
  • 🎁 Pairing this decision with initiatives in music content, premium live concerts and ad-supported products may give Netflix more ways to use its existing global membership base for incremental monetization, rather than betting on one very large transaction.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track how clearly Netflix links this decision to future capital deployment on earnings calls, including content budgets, AI-powered production tools, and share repurchases. It is also worth watching how the ad-supported tier scales in markets like Canada, how live and music programming perform, and whether commentary shifts as rivals such as Disney, Amazon and any combined Paramount Warner entity adjust their own streaming strategies. These signals will help you judge whether staying independent, with a partnership-heavy model, supports Netflix's long-term position in the streaming market after stepping away from Warner Bros. Discovery.

Approach 1: Moderna Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It looks at the cash the company might generate for shareholders rather than just current earnings.

For Moderna, the latest twelve month free cash flow (FCF) is a loss of about $2.19b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates used in this 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model suggest FCF remains negative for several years, then turns positive, reaching $254m in 2030. Further out, projections step up gradually into the mid hundreds of millions of dollars per year, all expressed in dollars and then discounted to today.

Putting these cash flow projections together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $27.64 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$53.54, the DCF output implies the stock is around 93.7% overvalued on this set of assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Moderna Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are limited or volatile, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E, because it compares the share price with the revenue being generated rather than current profits. Investors typically expect higher P/S multiples when they see stronger growth potential and lower perceived risk, and lower P/S multiples when growth expectations are softer or risks are higher.

Moderna currently trades on a P/S of 10.92x. This is close to the broader Biotechs industry average P/S of 10.84x, and above the peer group average of 4.74x. data.title: China Shengmu Organic Milk (SEHK:1432) Returns To Loss In 1H 2025 Challenging Profitability Hopes
data.description:
China Shengmu Organic Milk (SEHK:1432) opened FY 2025 with first half revenue of CNY 1,444.3 million and basic EPS of CNY 0.005844 loss per share, setting a cautious tone for investors focused on profitability. Over the last three reported half year periods, revenue moved from CNY 1,490.7 million in 1H 2024 to CNY 1,635.5 million in 2H 2024, then to CNY 1,444.3 million in 1H 2025. Basic EPS shifted from a CNY 0.017588 loss to CNY 0.00948, before settling at a CNY 0.005844 loss, leaving margins under pressure and putting the quality of any future improvement in the spotlight.

See our full analysis for China Shengmu Organic Milk.

Losses Widen To CNY 373.4m Over TTM

  • On a trailing twelve month view, net income excluding extra items moved from a CNY 65.5m loss to a CNY 373.4m loss, even though total revenue stayed around CNY 3.0b.
  • Bears focus on this pattern because it shows earnings falling faster than sales, with losses growing at about 59.8% per year over five years, which they argue points to business pressure rather than a short term bump in costs.
    • Critics highlight that even the most recent profitable trailing period, with CNY 29.9m of net income on CNY 3,079.8m of revenue, was followed by a return to a much larger loss.
    • This sequence heavily supports the bearish view that profitability has not yet settled into a stable, repeatable level.
On this track record, skeptics question whether recent half year improvements can offset multi year earnings pressure 🐻 China Shengmu Organic Milk Bear Case

Half Year Swings From CNY 78.2m Profit To CNY 48.3m Loss

  • Within three consecutive half year periods, net income excluding extra items went from a CNY 143.7m loss in 1H 2024 to a CNY 78.2m profit in 2H 2024, then back to a CNY 48.3m loss in 1H 2025.
  • What stands out for a bearish narrative is how these swings line up with EPS, moving from a CNY 0.017588 loss to a CNY 0.00948 profit, then to a CNY 0.005844 loss, which suggests earnings per share have not followed a consistent direction even as revenue stayed between CNY 1,444.3m and CNY 1,635.5m.
    • Skeptics point to this mix of profit and loss as backing the view that the business has not yet produced a clear, sustained earnings trend.
    • The tension for investors is that each period tells a different story, so it is hard to anchor expectations just on the latest half.

P/S Of 0.8x Despite Ongoing Losses

  • The shares trade on a P/S of 0.8x while the peer group averages 0.6x and the Hong Kong Food industry sits at 0.7x, even though trailing twelve month earnings are negative.
  • What is striking for a bearish case is that the premium P/S multiple sits beside a CNY 373.4m trailing twelve month loss and a CNY 0.045 loss per share, which bears argue makes the valuation sensitive to any further earnings weakness.
    • Critics argue that paying a higher P/S than peers is harder to justify while the company is still unprofitable on a trailing basis.
    • At a share price of HK$0.34 with no positive trailing EPS, the bear view is that valuation now leans more on hope than on recent profit delivery.

data.title: Margin Compression To 2.3% At C&D International (SEHK:1908) Tests Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
C&D International Investment Group (SEHK:1908) has posted its FY 2025 results with second half revenue of about C¥102.6b and basic EPS of C¥1.06, while trailing twelve month revenue stood at roughly C¥136.8b with EPS of C¥1.58. The group has seen revenue move from about C¥110.2b in 2H 2024 to C¥102.6b in 2H 2025, with basic EPS shifting from C¥1.86 to C¥1.06 over the same period, setting the stage for investors to weigh the latest trend in profitability. With profit margins sitting in the low single digits, the focus now turns to how durable those margins look against the changing earnings profile.

See our full analysis for C&D International Investment Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely followed narratives around growth, risk, and quality to see which views hold up and which might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Slip Back To 2.3%

  • Trailing net profit margin is 2.3% compared with 3.0% a year earlier, on trailing twelve month net income of about C¥3.2b from revenue of roughly C¥136.8b.
  • Bears focus on this margin compression, yet the record of five year earnings growth around 7.6% a year and past comments about high earnings quality create a tension between short term pressure and a longer track of profitability consistency.
    • Critics highlight that 2H 2025 net income of about C¥2.3b on C¥102.6b of revenue sits below 2H 2024 net income of roughly C¥3.5b on C¥110.2b of revenue, which lines up with the weaker margin figure.
    • At the same time, the multi year 7.6% annual earnings growth rate and the description of prior earnings as high quality challenge a purely bearish story that recent softness defines the whole business.

Revenue Trend Softens While EPS Stays Positive

  • Second half revenue moved from C¥110,232.9m in 2024 to C¥102,623.9m in 2025, while 2H basic EPS remained positive at C¥1.06 alongside trailing twelve month EPS of about C¥1.58.
  • What stands out for a cautiously bullish view is that, even as revenue is expected to decline about 1.5% a year over the next three years, forecasts point to earnings growth of roughly 23.2% a year, which sits side by side with the current run rate of C¥3.2b of trailing net income.
    • Supporters can point out that 1H 2025 and 2H 2025 together produced around C¥3.2b of net income in total, consistent with the trailing twelve month figure, so the company is still generating profits on a large revenue base.
    • On the other hand, the step down in 2H 2025 EPS from C¥1.86 in 2H 2024 to C¥1.06 shows why bulls need those forward growth estimates to materialize to back up the optimistic stance.

Low 8x P/E Versus 64% Upside Target

  • At a share price of HK$12.80 and a trailing P/E of 8x, the stock trades below the Hong Kong real estate industry average of 13.2x and the peer average of 28.7x, with an analyst target price of HK$21.01 and a DCF fair value of HK$100.13.
  • Supporters of a bullish angle argue that this mix of a lower than peer P/E, a DCF fair value well above the current price and analyst targets that imply roughly 64% upside suggests the market is not fully crediting the C¥3.2b of trailing earnings and the earnings growth estimates, even after factoring in the 2.3% margin and unstable dividend history.
    • The valuation gap is clear when the DCF fair value of HK$100.13 is compared with the HK$12.80 share price, which is a very large discount, while the 8x P/E sits below the 13.2x industry level.
    • Yet the weaker margin compared with last year and the description of the dividend record as unstable give bears specific data points to justify caution, even with an analyst target of HK$21.01 above the current price.

data.title: Maanshan Iron & Steel (SEHK:323) Q4 EPS Loss Tests Bulls’ Turnaround Narrative
data.description:
Maanshan Iron & Steel (SEHK:323) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of C¥19.95b and a basic EPS loss of C¥0.038, while trailing twelve month EPS sits at C¥0.03 on revenue of C¥77.52b and a net income loss of C¥208.81m. Over recent quarters, the company has seen revenue move from C¥20.30b and an EPS loss of C¥0.276 in Q4 2024 to C¥19.95b and a narrower EPS loss of C¥0.038 in Q4 2025, against a backdrop of trailing twelve month net losses that remain material. For investors, the headline story is that revenue is holding near the C¥20b per quarter mark while margins remain under pressure. This keeps the focus on whether future earnings momentum can rebuild profitability.

See our full analysis for Maanshan Iron & Steel.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely held narratives about Maanshan Iron & Steel's growth potential, risks, and expected turnaround.

See what the community is saying about Maanshan Iron & Steel

Advertisement

LTM loss of C¥208.8m despite four quarters near C¥19b to C¥20b revenue

  • Over the last twelve months, Maanshan Iron & Steel generated C¥77.5b in revenue and recorded a net income loss of C¥208.8m, after a year where quarterly revenue stayed in a tight C¥18.7b to C¥20.3b range.
  • Consensus narrative expects future earnings to reach C¥4.3b by about March 2029 with revenue of C¥81.9b and a 5.3% margin, yet the current trailing loss and low utilization concerns show how much has to change for that view to play out.
    • Recent quarters show swings between profit and loss, with Q3 2025 net income of C¥158.4m flipping back to a C¥292.4m loss in Q4 2025, which sits uneasily against a smooth improvement storyline.
    • Analysts also point to ongoing overcapacity and high raw material exposure, so the current loss profile highlights the execution risk behind the margin recovery they model.

When you compare what the company actually earned over the last year to where analysts expect margins and profits to land by 2029, you can see how much of the story still has to be delivered on the income statement.

EPS swings from C¥0.0208 to a loss of C¥0.038 challenge the bullish turnaround pace

  • Across FY 2025, basic EPS moved from a loss of C¥0.02 in Q1 to C¥0.008 and C¥0.0208 in Q2 and Q3, before slipping back to a C¥0.038 loss in Q4, even as trailing twelve month EPS sits at a small profit of C¥0.03.
  • Bullish investors point to forecast earnings growth of 96.63% per year and the expectation that Maanshan Iron & Steel becomes profitable within three years, yet the quarterly EPS pattern shows that profitability has not settled into a clear trend.
    • Losses over the past five years are reported to have grown at an annual rate of 58.5%, so the latest quarter’s return to a loss gives bulls more to prove than a simple one year turnaround story suggests.
    • At the same time, moving from a large Q4 2024 loss of C¥2.1b to a much smaller Q4 2025 loss of C¥292.4m does line up with the idea that the worst of the earnings pressure may already be reflected in trailing figures.

If you are weighing that optimistic path back to profitability, it helps to keep one eye on how consistently EPS holds above zero rather than just on the longer term growth percentages.

Bulls argue that cost reforms, special steel growth, and higher margin products could turn these patchy EPS numbers into steadier profits over time, while the recent loss history shows why the bar for that narrative is still high. 🐂 Maanshan Iron & Steel Bull Case

P/S of 0.2x and HK$2.53 price vs C¥10.3b DCF fair value support the cautious bear case

  • At a current share price of HK$2.53, Maanshan Iron & Steel is trading on a P/S of 0.2x compared with a peer average of 1.4x and an industry average of 0.7x, and against a DCF fair value of HK$10.30 per share.
  • Bears focus on the company’s trailing unprofitability and multi year loss expansion of 58.5% per year, arguing that even this low P/S and the gap to DCF fair value do not fully capture the risks from overcapacity, high input costs, and slower demand.
    • Forecast revenue growth of 2.2% per year is below the 8.2% forecast for the Hong Kong market, so part of the discount may be tied to the lower top line growth outlook that bears highlight.
    • Analyst price targets span from HK$1.96 on the bearish side to HK$2.85 on the bullish side, near a consensus of about HK$2.55, which is very close to the current HK$2.53 price and suggests the market is already treating the stock cautiously despite the DCF gap.

If you are leaning toward the cautious view, it is worth asking whether the wide gap between today’s low multiples and the DCF fair value is a genuine mispricing or simply reflects the earnings and industry risks already visible in the recent numbers. 🐻 Maanshan Iron & Steel Bear Case
